title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read an article about languages and the way humans communicate. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Architecture of Meaning
content.subtitle: An article about language, communication, and the diversity of human expression.
content.text:
Every language is, in a sense, a theory of the world. The words a language provides — and equally, the distinctions it does not bother to encode — reveal what its speakers have collectively found worth naming. [31] The well-known example of Inuit communities possessing numerous words for different types of snow is sometimes dismissed as exaggerated, yet the underlying principle holds: languages carve up reality in different ways, and those divisions reflect the priorities and environments of the people who developed them. [/31]

For much of the twentieth century, a dominant view in linguistics held that language shapes thought — that the words available to you determine, to some degree, what you are able to think. This hypothesis, associated with the linguists Sapir and Whorf, fell out of favour as evidence mounted that humans could conceive of things they lacked words for. [32] More recent research, however, has suggested a subtler version of the original claim: language may not constrain thought, but it does influence how readily certain thoughts are accessed, and how efficiently concepts are communicated to others. [/32]

The question of what language does to thought is, in any case, somewhat separate from what is happening to the world's languages right now. [33] Of the approximately seven thousand languages currently spoken on Earth, linguists estimate that roughly half will be extinct by the end of this century. When a language disappears, it typically does so quietly: the last fluent speakers age and die, while younger generations, drawn towards dominant languages by economic necessity and social pressure, let the ancestral tongue fade. [/33]

What is lost when a language dies? Some argue that the loss is primarily sentimental — that the essential content of human thought can always be translated. Others insist that untranslatable concepts, embedded in endangered languages, represent genuinely distinct ways of perceiving the world. [34] There is also the matter of ecological knowledge: many smaller languages encode detailed information about local plants, animals, and environments — information accumulated over centuries that has no equivalent in scientific literature. [/34]

The rise of English as a global lingua franca has provoked ambivalent feelings among linguists and cultural commentators. Undeniably, it has made international communication vastly easier and has opened doors for millions of people. [35] At the same time, the dominance of any single language risks creating a kind of monoculture of expression — a narrowing of the range of concepts and metaphors through which human experience is filtered. [/35] The concern is not simply romantic; it has practical implications for creativity, diplomacy, and problem-solving.

Efforts to preserve endangered languages have had mixed results. Some revitalisation projects have succeeded impressively — Welsh and Māori being frequently cited examples — but most require sustained political will and community engagement that is difficult to maintain. [36] Language, ultimately, cannot be saved by legislation alone; it requires speakers who feel it is worth the effort of passing on, which means it must remain alive to the needs and identities of those who use it. [/36]

questions.question: What point does the author make using the example of Inuit snow vocabulary?
options: A) Popular examples about language are often factually incorrect.
B) Arctic communities have more complex languages than others.
C) Different languages reflect the particular concerns of their speakers.
D) The number of words in a language is related to its complexity.
questions.explanation: The author uses the example to support the principle that 'languages carve up reality in different ways, and those divisions reflect the priorities and environments of the people who developed them'.

questions.question: Recent research into the relationship between language and thought suggests that
options: A) Sapir and Whorf's original hypothesis has been entirely disproved.
B) language affects how easily we think certain thoughts, not what we can think.
C) people who speak more languages can think more clearly.
D) the vocabulary we have determines the limits of our intelligence.
questions.explanation: Recent research proposes 'a subtler version': language 'may not constrain thought, but it does influence how readily certain thoughts are accessed'.

questions.question: The author states that when languages disappear, it is usually because
options: A) governments actively suppress minority languages.
B) younger speakers choose dominant languages for practical reasons.
C) the last speakers move away from their communities.
D) written records of the language are lost or destroyed.
questions.explanation: Younger generations are described as being 'drawn towards dominant languages by economic necessity and social pressure', while the ancestral tongue fades.

questions.question: One reason given for why the loss of a language is significant is that
options: A) translations between languages are never fully accurate.
B) endangered languages are more grammatically complex than dominant ones.
C) smaller languages may contain irreplaceable environmental knowledge.
D) untranslatable words are always the most important in a language.
questions.explanation: The text specifically mentions that 'many smaller languages encode detailed information about local plants, animals, and environments' with 'no equivalent in scientific literature'.

questions.question: What concern does the author raise about the dominance of English?
options: A) It has made international diplomacy significantly more difficult.
B) It may reduce the variety of ways in which human experience is expressed.
C) It discourages people from learning other foreign languages.
D) It gives native English speakers an unfair advantage in global business.
questions.explanation: The author warns of 'a kind of monoculture of expression — a narrowing of the range of concepts and metaphors through which human experience is filtered'.

questions.question: The author's view on language preservation is that
options: A) only government funding can save endangered languages.
B) revitalisation projects have generally been unsuccessful.
C) a language survives only if its speakers find it relevant to their lives.
D) technology will eventually make language extinction less likely.
questions.explanation: The final sentence states a language 'requires speakers who feel it is worth the effort of passing on' and 'must remain alive to the needs and identities of those who use it'.
